entry.title: Introducing Key Performance Indicator (KPI) Monitoring in PP/DS for SAP S/4HANA
entry.content:
Background

SAP S/4HANA Manufacturing for planning and scheduling (PP/DS) capability is used to create procurement proposals for in-house production or external procurement to cover product requirements and to optimize and plan the resource schedule and the order dates/times in detail. PP/DS helps a Production Planner to reduce the lead times, to increase on-time delivery performance, or to increase the throughput of products and reduce the stock costs.

However, how would the Production Planner know whether their production plan is really helping them achieve their goals or not?

Depending on the complexity of the business, a production planner could be dealing with hundreds of resources, thousands of products, and thousands of partners and customers. Monitoring the Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) for complex production plans is critical to ensure that the production plan is feasible and is aligned with business goals.

 

Introduction

As part of the SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) release 2024.Q4, a new Business Content Package for Line of Business (LoB) titled "Production Planning and Detailed Scheduling" is released. This Business Content Package includes 4 stories -

  • Order Details
  • Order Operations
  • Pegging Relationships (Order to Demand)
  • Resource Capacity

The above SAC stories need a live data connection to SAP S/4HANA 2023 system. The four Analytical Queries released as part of SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS2 serve as the data source for these stories

Once you have successfully created the connection between your SAC tenant and your SAP S/4HANA system, the SAC stories would display the KPIs and charts based on your data.

 

SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) stories

Order Details

The SAP PP/DS: KPI Monitoring - Order Details story has an Overview and a Details page, and displays important performance indicators about your orders, such as the:

  • number of orders
  • number of delayed orders
  • lead, processing, queue, setup, and wait time
  • number of schedule lines
  • confirmed, original, receipt, scrap, and total quantity

Figure 1: Order Details - OverviewFigure 2: Order Details - Details

This story also links to and from the Order Operations and Pegging Relationships (Order to Demand) stories.

Order Operations

The SAP PP/DS: KPI Monitoring - Order Operations story has an Overview page and displays important performance indicators about the operations of your orders, such as the:

  • number of operations
  • number of sub operations
  • gross operation duration
  • net operation duration

Figure 3: Order Operations - Overview

This story also links to and from the Order Details and Resource Capacity stories.

Pegging Relationships (Order to Demand)

The SAP PP/DS: KPI Monitoring - Pegging Relationships (Order to Demand) story has an Overview and a Details page, and displays important performance indicators about your orders and their pegging relationships, such as the:

  • service level percentage
  • maximum earliness and lateness time
  • total buffer time
  • early, on-time, delayed, and total relationships count
  • requirement quantity

Figure 4: Pegging Relationships (Order to Demand) - OverviewFigure 5: Pegging Relationships (Order to Demand) - Details

This story also links to and from the Order Details story.

Resource Capacity

The SAP PP/DS: KPI Monitoring - Resource Capacity story has an Overview and a Details page, and displays important performance indicators about your resources, such as the:

  • number of overloaded resources
  • capacity utilization
  • capacity required for setup, teardown, production, and queue

Figure 6: Resource Capacity - OverviewFigure 7: Resource Capacity - Details

This story also links to and from the Order Operations story.

 

Wrapping Up

The SAC stories for KPI Monitoring in PP/DS can be used to track and monitor the KPIs based on your current production plan. You can use these SAC stories "as is", or by fine-tuning them or by using them as reference to create your own SAC stories. 

Please feel free to share your feedback and comments.

P.S.: Refer SAP Note 3495936 - Restrictions and Implementation Recommendations for Analytics in PP/DS on SAP S/4HANA 2023 to ensure all the pre-requisites are maintained in your SAP S/4HANA system.

entry.title: Account determination for entry INT GBB ____ BSA 3120 not possible
entry.content:
While performing goods receipt using transaction MIGO, someone system though an error i.e. "Account determination for entry INT GBB ____ BSA 3120 not possible"

 

To mitigate this issue, execute transaction OBYC and then double click on GBB and then Pass Chart of Accounts INT and maintain the missing entry by copying existing entry.

entry.title: My First Experience with LeanIX: A Newcomer’s Guide to EA Modeling After getting Certification
entry.content:
As someone new to the field of Enterprise Architecture (EA), transitioning from a core solutions and technical architecture background, I quickly realized how different and strategic EA is compared to my previous role. In my previous role as a technical architect, I focused on designing and implementing specific solutions, but EA required a broader view—aligning IT systems with business strategies and managing the entire IT landscape.

To make this transition smoother, I began by obtaining my TOGAF EA Foundation certification, which provided me with a solid framework and understanding of the principles, methodologies, and processes behind Enterprise Architecture. Shortly after, I pursued LeanIX certification to gain hands-on knowledge in a platform that could help me manage, model, and optimize our IT landscape in line with business goals.

In this blog, I’ll share how my background as a technical architect, bolstered by TOGAF EA Foundation certification and LeanIX certification, helped me successfully navigate the world of Enterprise Architecture, and how LeanIX became a powerful tool in my journey.

What is LeanIX?

LeanIX is an advanced Enterprise Architecture Management (EAM) tool designed to help EA professionals model, visualize, and manage their organization’s IT architecture in alignment with business needs. Coming from a technical architecture background, I was used to focusing on individual systems and solutions. However, LeanIX allowed me to see the bigger picture of how all IT components are interconnected and how they align with the organization’s long-term business strategy.

After getting certified, I was ready to dive deeper into how LeanIX could help me structure our architecture models. The certification gave me a solid foundation in the platform’s capabilities and prepared me to leverage it fully.

Why LeanIX for Enterprise Architecture?

Having transitioned from a core solutions and technical architect role, I was looking for a tool that could:

  1. Centralize IT Information: As a technical architect, I was used to working with isolated systems. In EA, I needed to connect the dots between various IT components, so a centralized platform like LeanIX was perfect for organizing everything.
  2. Foster Collaboration: In my previous role, I primarily collaborated with developers and technical teams. EA, however, requires collaboration with business stakeholders, so I needed a tool that could bridge the gap between IT and business.
  3. Simplify Risk Management: As a technical architect, I often worked on the specifics of individual systems. EA, on the other hand, required me to assess the health of our entire IT landscape. LeanIX helped me identify risks and technical debt, ensuring our systems were aligned with the overall business strategy.

LeanIX helped me see the bigger picture, understand the relationships between IT components, and build a clear strategy for future improvements. 

For example, in the chemical industry use case, I was able to assess the organization’s global supply chain management system, integrating various business units such as production, procurement, and distribution, to ensure seamless collaboration across different regions and processes. This level of integration was key to improving the overall strategy.

Step 1: Defining the IT Landscape

As someone new to EA, one of my first tasks was to understand the current state of our IT architecture. LeanIX made it simple to define and visualize our entire IT landscape. Using the platform, I could create models of our business capabilities, applications, data flows, and technologies.

In the demo leading chemical industry use case, I began by defining the core business processes that were critical for the company’s success, such as supply chain management, production scheduling, and inventory management. The Application Portfolio feature was particularly valuable for understanding the relationships between applications and how they contributed to these critical business processes. I could easily categorize and assess applications, helping me identify redundancies and areas where we could improve efficiency.

  • Example: I mapped out how the Global ERP System interacted with other core business applications like procurement management, inventory management, and quality control systems. This helped identify redundant applications that could be consolidated, which contributed to reducing overall costs.

Step 2: Mapping Business Capabilities

A significant shift in my thinking, after my TOGAF certification, was realizing the importance of mapping business capabilities to the IT systems that support them. As a technical architect, I was focused on technology, but as Enterprise Architect, I needed to understand how IT systems drive business outcomes.

In LeanIX, I could easily map business capabilities to their respective supporting applications and technologies. This allowed me to understand how each piece of the IT landscape contributed to business objectives, and where improvements were needed.

For example, in the chemical industry use case, mapping capabilities such as Order Management and Product Lifecycle Management (PLM) to applications such as SAP S/4HANA and PLM solutions enabled me to understand the strengths and weaknesses in current operations.

  • Example: I mapped the Supply Chain Optimization business capability to the SAP S/4HANA and its integration with production and logistics. This process helped identify areas where improvements could be made, such as streamlining inventory tracking and improving demand forecasting.

Step 3: Assessing Risks and Technical Debt

In my previous technical role, I focused on specific applications and troubleshooting technical issues. However, after diving into Enterprise Architecture, I realized the importance of assessing risks and technical debt across the entire IT ecosystem. LeanIX’s Risk and Health Dashboard became a crucial tool in helping me prioritize these areas.

In the chemical industry use case, I focused on assessing the risk of outdated systems and fragmented data across multiple geographies, which could lead to inefficiencies in global supply chain management. The dashboard provided me with a comprehensive view of where risks existed in our applications, whether it was due to outdated technology, insufficient integrations, or lack of alignment with business needs. With my combined knowledge from EA and LeanIX, I could assess the entire landscape from a strategic perspective.

  • Example: I discovered that several legacy supply chain systems were still in use, which posed risks for scalability and integration with newer technologies. LeanIX helped me prioritize these systems for modernization and integration with cloud-based solutions.

Step 4: Creating a Roadmap for the Future

As EA, creating a strategic roadmap for IT transitions was an exciting challenge. I needed to think long-term about how our IT systems would evolve.  LeanIX provided the perfect platform to model future states of our IT landscape.

LeanIX’s roadmap feature allowed me to apply these principles and visualize how we would transition from legacy systems to modern, scalable solutions.

For example, in the chemical industry use case, I created a roadmap for the transition from on-premise, legacy SAP systems to cloud-based solutions to improve scalability and integration with other digital platforms. This migration plan was mapped out over several years, taking into account business priorities and resource availability.

  • Example: I planned the migration of inventory management systems to a cloud-based platform, ensuring a seamless transition without disrupting ongoing operations. This roadmap outlined clear milestones and timelines for upgrading systems across global locations.

Step 5: Collaborating Across Teams

A significant shift in my role from technical architect to Enterprise Architect was the need for collaboration across different departments and teams. LeanIX made it easier to communicate and collaborate with both IT and business stakeholders.

For example, in the chemical industry use case, I might work with business leaders from procurement, sales, and manufacturing to review and refine the IT architecture, ensuring it aligned with their operational goals and business needs.

  • Example: I could collaborate with the procurement team to ensure that the supply chain management system supported their requirements, while also working with the sales and production teams to ensure the integration of order fulfillment and production scheduling was optimized for better cross-functional collaboration.

Key Features I Found Useful as a New LeanIX User After Certification:

  1. Application Portfolio Management (APM): Helped me organize and assess the health of our IT applications across the organization, especially for supply chain and inventory management systems.
  2. Business Capability Mapping: Allowed me to align key business processes like order management and production planning with their supporting technologies.
  3. Risk and Health Dashboard: Provided a comprehensive view of risks and technical debt across the IT landscape, particularly for legacy systems in the global supply chain.
  4. Roadmap Creation: Enabled me to create a strategic roadmap for transitioning legacy systems to more modern, cloud-based solutions.
  5. Collaboration and Sharing: Simplified communication and alignment across various teams, especially when it came to procurement, manufacturing, and IT operations.

My Thoughts

As a first-time user, I found LeanIX to be an incredibly intuitive and powerful tool for enterprise architecture modeling. It provided the structure, tools, and insights I needed to understand our IT landscape better, identify areas for improvement, and align our technology with the broader business strategy.

With its user-friendly interface and powerful features, LeanIX will help you take your enterprise architecture practice to the next level. Whether you're documenting your current state, planning for the future, or identifying risks and opportunities, LeanIX is a game-changer for anyone starting out in the field of Enterprise Architecture.

entry.title: Outbound process with SAP EWM: POSC, WOCR & Wave Management
entry.content:
Outbound logistics is a critical component of supply chain management, ensuring products are delivered to customers efficiently and accurately. In #SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM), advanced tools like Process-Oriented Storage Control (POSC), Warehouse Order Creation Rules (WOCR), and Wave Management enable seamless execution of outbound processes. This blog provides an overview of a configured scenario to manage a complex outbound process, highlighting each component's role. 

Scenario Overview:

1. Customer Orders and Sales Order Creation:

  • Customer orders are received and processed in SAP.
  • A Sales Order is created in SAP S/4HANA based on customer requirements.
  • An Outbound Delivery is generated against the Sales Order in S/4HANA and distributed to SAP EWM as an Outbound Delivery Order (ODO).

2. Wave Management:

  • The actual EWM process begins upon the creation of the ODO.
  • In this scenario, Waves are created Automatically. A Wave groups multiple Outbound Delivery Orders
    based on specific criteria. For this case, the grouping condition is the Customer.
  • A Wave consolidates all ODOs associated with a single customer, facilitating streamlined operations.

3. Warehouse Order Creation Rules (WOCR):

  • WOCR is used to group Warehouse Tasks into Warehouse Orders based on predefined filters and limits.
  • It also supports the automatic creation of Pick Handling Units (Pick HUs) via packing profiles.
  • In this scenario, three WOCRs are configured for different resources:
    • Human Resources
    • Trolley
    • Forklift

4. Process-Oriented Storage Control (POSC):

  • POSC defines the sequential steps required to execute the outbound process.
  • In this scenario, the process includes:
  1. Picking: Pick Handling Units (HUs) are created during picking and moved to the packing work center.
  2. Packing: Products are packed by warehouse associates at the work center. Upon confirmation, an automatic Warehouse Task is created to move products to the Goods Issue (GI) staging area.
  3. Loading: Tasks are generated and confirmed to load products onto the delivery vehicle.
  4. Goods Issue: Final Goods Issue is performed, generating an Outbound Delivery Document in the system.

Upon Goods Issue, Post Processing Framework (PPF) actions are triggered to send the required information back to S/4HANA.

Configuration

This configuration focuses on the key steps, intentionally omitting basic configurations for the sake of brevity. It encompasses three critical components: Wave Management, WOCR, and POSC setup.

I.  Wave Management

As previously mentioned, this configuration involves auto wave creation using PPF Actions and PPF.

1. Maintain Wave Type
2. Maintain Wave Categories
3. Set Automatic Wave Generation for Warehouse Process Type
3.1 PPF Configuration

T.Code: SPPFCADM

Application

/SCDL/DELIVERY

Action Profile

/SCWM/PDO  01

Action Definition

/SCWM/PDO_01_WAVE

Method

/SCWM/WHR_WAVE_ASSIGN_NEW

Make sure Action Definition listed above active for AUTO Wave Generation.

3.2  Outbound Delivery Process Document Types
4. Define Condition Table
5. Define Access Sequence
6. Define Condition Types
7. Define Determination Procedure
8. Assign Procedure to Document type

9. Define Condition Maintenance Group
10. Maintain Wave Template

 
11. Maintain Wave Condition Record

II. Warehouse Order Creation Rule (WOCR)

In the context of WOCR, specific requirements have been defined based on the handling capacities of different resources:

  • WOC1: This rule applies to human resources where the load restriction is a maximum of 2 kilograms, suitable for items that can be carried manually by hand.
  • WOC2: This rule is designated for trolley-based operations, allowing a load capacity of up to 30 kilograms, making it ideal for medium-sized items that require mechanical assistance for movement.
  • WOC3: This rule is intended for heavy-duty equipment capable of managing loads up to 500 kilograms, suitable for large or bulky items that demand specialized handling equipment.

For detailed information and additional context regarding these requirements, please refer to the introduction section provided earlier. The basic configurations, such as number ranges for Warehouse Orders and sorting rules like "PIPA - Pick Path," have already been set up in the system. Let’s focus on the key steps involved in configuring WOCR.

1. Define Filters for warehouse order creation rule

 

2. Define Limits values for the size of a warehouse order

3. Define packing profile for Warehouse order Creation

3.1 Packaging Specification

Packaging specifications are a prerequisite for the packing profile. When creating a Pick HU, the system uses these specifications to determine the appropriate packaging material.

3.2. Assign Bins to Activity Area

Virtual bins are created for each activity area in advance and assigned accordingly. This step  is crucial for the creation of Pick HUs.

3.3 Warehouse process type for pick HU

3.4 Assign WPT to warehouse as WPT HU as Res

4. Define Creation Rule for warehouse Orders

5. Define Search Sequence of creation rules for activity areas

III. Process- Oriented Storage Control (POSC)

The POSC workflow involves the following steps: Upon the release of a wave, picking warehouse tasks are automatically generated, and pick HUs are simultaneously created. These pick HUs are then moved to the designated packing work center, where items are prepared for further processing. Following packing, the items are staged appropriately and subsequently loaded for shipment.

The Warehouse Process Type (WPT) "ZPIC" has already been configured in the system, and corresponding stock removal strategies have been established in alignment with the ZPIC WPT. These configurations ensure that stock removal processes are executed efficiently and adhere to the specific requirements associated with the defined WPT.

1. External Storage process

2. Define Storage process

3. Assign Storage process step

4. Process Oriented Storage Control

Assign storage process steps to their designated locations, such as the staging step to the storage type and the packing step to the corresponding work center.

IV. Work Center

The packing process requires a dedicated work center, for which the layout "VPMX" has been utilized. The standard storage type designated for the packing work center is 8030, and a corresponding bin has been created within the 8030-storage type to facilitate smooth packing operations.

1. Define Work Center

2. Assign Storage bin to Work Center

Test case:

Let’s consider a scenario where orders are received from customers. For instance, two customers place orders for three different products separately. The business requirement is to group these orders based on customers, which is referred to as a wave.

Once the wave is created, the WOCR comes into play. The configuration is set to be released immediately, warehouse tasks are automatically generated and grouped into warehouse orders based on the available resources. In this configuration, resources are categorized as Human, Trolley, and Forklift for picking operations. Additionally, Pick HUs are created automatically during this process.

Warehouse associates perform these tasks and confirm them using RF devices. The workflow then proceeds to move the picked products to the packing area, followed by staging and loading, and finally to the Goods Issue. Each subsequent task is created automatically once the previous task is confirmed which will be executed by the POSC. For simplicity, this blog demonstrates all processes using the Monitor instead of RF. In real-world scenarios, these processes are typically executed via RF in real time.

1. Sales Order Creation (VA01)

10 Sales Orders are created with two different customers for 3 products as shown in the table below. 24-2550(5Q) represents 24 is the Customer, 2550 is the products and 5Q in parenthesis is Quantity.

Sales Order

Customer-Products

Sales Order

Customer- Products

6127

24-2550(5Q)

6126

27-2565(2Q)

6132

24-2551(4Q)

6124

27-2550(30Q)

6133

24-2565(1Q)

6125

27-2551(2Q)

6130

24-2551(10Q)

6134

27-2565(1Q)

6128

24-2550(1Q)

6129

27-2550(2Q)

2. Outbound delivery creation in S/4 (VL01N)

Against Sales Order Created in the S/4, Inbound deliveries will be created, those are listed in the table below.

Sales Order

Inbound Delivery

Sales Order

Inbound Delivery

6124

80005863

6129

80005868

6125

80005864

6130

80005869

6126

80005865

6132

80005870

6127

80005866

6133

80005871

6128

80005867

6134

80005872

All outbound deliveries have been created and successfully distributed to EWM.

3. Outbound Delivery Order in EWM (/N/SCWM/PRDO)

Let’s check whether Outbound deliveries are created and distributed to EWM. Since 10 Outbound deliveries created, searched with advanced search.

Auto Wave Creation:

Waves can be created manually using T-code /N/SCWM/WAVE, but Auto Wave Creation enabled (refer the configuration step I. 3). This process starts with the WPT, triggering PPF actions to generate waves using wave templates 101 and 102, based on condition records configured earlier (refer configuration I. 11). The system created two waves: 5039 for Business Partner 148 and 5040 for Business Partner 129. The PPF log (see image) illustrates the wave creation process as per configuration (refer configuration step I. 3.1). Let’s get into the warehouse Monitor for wider visibility.

4. Wave Release (/N/SCWM/MON)

Two waves were created immediately upon delivery distribution, and they were automatically released. The wave status is marked as "R" (Released), and the wave release method is "B" (Immediate Release), as defined in the wave template. The configuration ensures that outbound delivery orders are grouped by the Ship-to Party, with one wave created for each customer. For instance, outbound deliveries for customer 129 were grouped into a single wave, as shown in the image.

The setup ensures that waves are automatically released for picking tasks as soon as they are created. Alternatively, manual wave release is also possible through the Warehouse Monitor or T-code /N/SCWM/WAVE, depending on the configuration in the wave template.

Warehouse Order Creation Rule (WOCR):

Next, let’s explore the steps following wave release by focusing on a single wave to understand how warehouse operations are executed. Once the wave is released, the system immediately refers to the Warehouse Order Creation Rules (WOCR) for creating warehouse orders.

In this example, three WOCRs were configured, and each was triggered based on the defined filters and weight limits. As shown in the image, the system created the following warehouse orders:WOC1: 2 kg, WOC2: Three orders of 5 kg, 5 kg, and 25 kg and WOC3: 150 kg.

These ranges are clearly defined in the WOCR configuration, where the link between the wave and WOCR is established through the wave category specified in the WOCR filters (refer to the configuration step- II.1).

Warehouse Task creation:

After WOCR processing, the focus shifts to the Warehouse Tasks assigned under the WO. These tasks are created to move stock from the picking storage type to the packing work center, as defined in the configuration.

This process follows the POSC configuration. As shown in the image, the WPT is ZPIC, with the Storage Process and External Step both triggered as ZPIC. The POSC is activated via WOCR, as the storage process is linked in the WOCR determination (refer to configuration step II.4). Picking operations are entirely driven by stock removal strategies, which play a crucial role in the creation of warehouse tasks for picking. However, detailed steps for stock removal strategies are omitted in this blog to maintain its brevity. It’s important to note that understanding these strategies is essential for efficient picking processes in the warehouse.

Let’s confirm picking warehouse tasks…

Auto Pick HU:

Upon confirmation of the warehouse tasks, the destination bin automatically changed to "VIRTUAL_BIN1". This behavior is linked to the Pick Handling Unit (Pick HU) configuration. The system is set to automatically create Pick HUs during the picking process, as defined in the WOCR Packing Profile (refer configuration step- II.3).

Based on the packaging specifications configured in the system, the system assigned the appropriate packaging material to each task. As shown in the image, a unique Pick HU number was created for each WT, ensuring proper handling and alignment with the configuration.

Next, let’s verify whether follow-up tasks were created to move the products from the VIRTUAL_BIN1 to the Packing Work Center for packing. To identify these subsequent tasks,  filter for Open WT with the WPT set to 3070, as specified in the POSC configuration (refer to configuration step III.4).

Subsequent tasks are created based on the external step "ZPAC" with WPT 3070, as configured in the POSC. Confirming these tasks moves the products to the Packing Work Center, which is linked to storage type 8030 and an assigned storage bin (refer to configuration step IV).

After confirming these tasks, no further tasks are generated immediately, as the system awaits a completion update from the work center. Once the system receives this update, it automatically creates the next set of tasks to move the products out of the work center. To update the completion status, the transaction code /N/SCWM/PACK is used.

Packing Work Center:

The "PACK" work center is configured as the designated Packing Work Center using the relevant T-code. In this interface, SAP provides a variety of features, including creating packages, repacking, and other functionalities, to pack products according to customer requirements.

Staging:

Upon completing the process step in the Packing Work Center, the system generates additional tasks with the external step "ZSTG", as illustrated in the image below.

These tasks are created to move the products from the Packing Work Center to the Goods Issue (GI) Staging Area. Let’s proceed to confirm these tasks as well.

Loading:

Once these tasks are confirmed, another set of tasks is generated to move the products from the Staging Area to the Door, completing the loading process. This marks the conclusion of the POSC workflow.

Let’s repeat the same process for the other wave, although the detailed steps are omitted in this blog for brevity. As mentioned earlier, in real-world scenarios, these tasks are not executed in the Monitor but are performed using RF devices. Finally, let’s check the status of the outbound deliveries in the PRDO transaction.

Goods Issue:

As shown in the image, the status of all outbound deliveries has been updated to Picking Completed, Packing Completed, and Loading Completed, indicating successful execution of the POSC process. By selecting Goods Issue, the system completes the goods issue for these outbound deliveries. Once the goods issue is completed, an outbound delivery document is created, triggering PPF actions to transfer this information to the ERP system.

Outbound delivery Document:

Outbound delivery Document created and PPF actions triggered to pass information to the ERP system as expected.

Let’s check whether the information passed to S/4..

T.code : VL03N-  S/4 Display Outbound Delivery

To illustrate the process,  verifying and displaying one of the outbound deliveries in this blog.

The Goods Issue information has been successfully updated in S/4HANA, as indicated by the Goods Movement Status marked as "C" (Completed). The system also triggered the Movement Type 601 in S/4, confirming the successful transfer of goods.

Conclusion

This blog explored the seamless execution of warehouse processes using Process-Oriented Storage Control (POSC) in SAP EWM. Starting with Auto Wave Creation & Release and Warehouse Order Creation Rules (WOCR), demonstrated how tasks are efficiently grouped and executed. The flow covered picking, packing, and goods movement from the Picking Storage Type to the Packing Work Center, Goods Issue Staging Area, and finally to the Door for loading.

Key configurations, such as Pick HUs and task automation, were highlighted, along with the integration of EWM and S/4HANA, where updates like Goods Movement Status and Movement Type 601 ensure real-time synchronization. While RF execution and repetitive steps were omitted for brevity, this blog showcased SAP EWM’s ability to optimize warehouse operations efficiently.

entry.title: SAP Field Logistics: Enhancing Rental Onboarding and Monitoring through SAP Fieldglass Integration
entry.content:
SAP Field Logistics is a solution built on SAP S/4HANA. It is available on S/4HANA Private Cloud, Public Cloud and On-Premise Editions. It allows customers to utilise best-in-class industry practices to manage logistics movement between their supply and demand plants.

SAP Fieldglass is a cloud-based vendor management system that streamlines procurement by managing external talent and services. It integrates effectively with SAP S/4HANA through SAP Business Network, offering a robust, best-practice service procurement process. This integration gives customers complete control and visibility over their source-to-pay process for services, ensuring seamless vendor collaboration.

For more information on the Service Procurement Integration, refer to the SAP help link: Integrations in the Sourcing and Procurement

Supplier Item Onboarding using Fieldglass

Customers frequently rent equipment for offshore operations, with demand typically linked to requests for service operations. Vendors usually recommend the necessary rental equipment and additional items, such as toolboxes, to support the job. ( These rental and third-party items are commonly referred to as Supplier Items). Efficient handling requires onboarding these supplier items with the service, monitoring their logistics, and ensuring timely offboarding.

Therefore, Field Logistics provides a ready-made standard integration between SAP Fieldglass and Field Logistics to facilitate the exchange of information about these items. This integration is designed with service procurement integration as a prerequisite, enhancing service procurement with additional tracking and management of rental and third-party items.

( Note: A reference business synopsis can be found here: Supplier Equipment Monitoring Business Synopsis )

Below is the high-level outline of the integration.

Integration Setup

1. Setup of the Service Procurement with SAP Business Network and SAP Fieldglass (4R2)

The customer must set up their system first for service procurement, following the best practices content for scope item 4R2. Refer to the documentation below to understand and set up the scenario.

Help info: Service Procurement with SAP Business Network and SAP Fieldglass (4R2)

Setting Up Service Procurement with SAP Business Network and SAP Fieldglass (4R2)

Config Guide: Services Procurement Implementation Guide

2. SAP Fieldglass setup for Supplier Item integration

The Fieldglass system must be set up with additional fields and connectors to make it ready for integration. The document below covers the setup steps to be performed.

Config Guide: Supplier Equipment Monitoring Implementation setup

3. Setup of the SAP Cloud Integration content

Standard integration flows facilitate information mapping and handover between SAP Fieldglass and Field Logistics systems. The customer must activate the content and perform an initial setup to enable the integration.

Config Guide: Configure the Integration flows and credentials

4. Setting up the Logistics execution for Supplier Items (4AH)

Customers must activate the Field Logistics process in the system to enable the logistics processes for supplier items. The setup guide below must be followed and completed to enable the best practice for scope item 4AH.

Config Guide: Setting Up Field Logistics Planning and Execution – Supplier Items (4AH)

Key Benefits

  1. Enhanced Visibility: Gain complete oversight of rental and third-party item lifecycles, including status updates and progress tracking.
  2. Improved Efficiency: Streamline the process of onboarding and managing rental and third-party items, reducing manual effort and errors.
  3. Accurate Cost Tracking: Ensure that the actual spending for billable days aligns with the required amounts, preventing overspending or underutilisation.
  4. Seamless Collaboration: Provide a centralised platform for managing rental and third-party items to facilitate collaboration between field service teams and suppliers.

Reference Information for SAP Field Logistics:

SAP Help Documentation:

SAP Field Logistics for any-premise

SAP Field Logistics for the public cloud

SAP Best practice content for S/4HANA Cloud for Supplier Item:

Supplier Item scope - 4AH

SAP Best practice content for S/4HANA Cloud for other Field Logistics scope:

Base Field Logistics Scope - 40G

Non-stock Item scope - 5OM

Container and Voyages – 6BA

Field Logistics Analytics – 6VA

 

Review Credits: Content reviewed and enriched by Field Logistics product and the UA team

entry.title: Why choose SAP Business Network Global Track and Trace over individual visibility platforms (1 of 7)
entry.content:
Here we are starting a series of seven posts taking a closer look at why supply chain and logistics professionals leverage SAP Business Network Global Track and Trace for greater logistics visibility and supply chain resilience, as compared to individual external shipment tracking platforms. Each post in this series dives into one of the seven key points highlighted here, providing examples relevant to supply chain, fulfillment, and logistics operations working across localized or global markets.

Reason #1: Connect Once, Collaborate with Many

Seamless collaboration is essential to logistics success, driving much of the efficiency needed to be competitive. However, for manufacturing and distribution companies with sprawling ecosystems of suppliers, carriers, and service providers, managing multiple individual partner connections can quickly become a technical and administrative trap that is hard to escape. Each entity often operates on its own systems, offering other partners the opportunity to integrate with their system, but missing the bigger picture of a common platform that integrates natively with the manufacturers ERP foundation.  Collaboration through a growing list of point-to-point integrations leads to a tangled web of costly technical maintenance and time-consuming training. This not only places a significant burden on IT resources but also hinders agility and responsiveness among core logistics operations.

SAP Business Network Global Track and Trace offers a transformative approach. By connecting once to this network, a manufacturer gains access to an ecosystem of partners, all communicating through a standardized platform and data format. This eliminates the need for numerous custom integrations, not only significantly reducing IT complexity and costs, but also reducing training and swivel-chair processes between portals and systems external to core ERP and securely managed data landscapes. Instead of grappling with disparate systems, SAP’s solutions enable manufacturers to focus on streamlining operations and fostering collaboration, from their ERP and secure corporate system landscape.

For instance, consider a manufacturer sourcing components from various suppliers across continents, or fulfilling customer orders with guaranteed short lead-times. Using SAP's solution, they can seamlessly track shipments from each supplier or to each customer, regardless of their location or carrier, without exporting core business information for the essential business context of a shipment, and without logging into any external systems. This unified view allows for optimized planning and execution, proactive monitoring, and native impact assessment if any shipment delays. 

By employing the "Connect Once, Collaborate with Many" principle, global manufacturing companies can unlock the true potential of their logistics networks. This streamlined approach not only simplifies IT management but also empowers them to build stronger relationships with their partners, enhancing efficiency and responsiveness across the entire supply chain. Stay tuned for our next post, where we'll explore how SAP's solution tackles the challenge of handling disparate data sources.

 

Read more about how SAP customer BSH leverages SAP Business Network Global Track and Trace for customer satisfaction in their fulfillment process. 

Next up, reason #2: Handling Disparate Data Sources.  Check back here for the upcoming link or subscribe to SAP Business Network for Logistics on SAP Community.

entry.title: #10 - Review Cutover Plan - And The Importance for a Successful Go-Live
entry.content:
A successful project Go-Live is a significant milestone for any organization, marking the transition from legacy systems to a new, integrated SAP environment. The cutover phase is the final step before the system is fully operational, and the process requires meticulous planning, preparation, and execution. One of the most critical components in achieving a smooth transition is having a well-organized and comprehensive Cutover Plan.

Image 1: Cutover Overview within SAP Activate Phases

A cutover plan is a detailed, step-by-step strategy designed to ensure a smooth transition from a legacy system to a new system. It outlines all the activities and tasks that need to be completed during the cutover phase—the period right before and after the system goes live. This plan is essential to minimize disruptions, ensure data accuracy, and enable users to adopt the new system effectively.

Key Components of a Cutover Plan:

  1. Pre-Cutover Activities:
    • Data migration and validation to ensure data is accurately transferred to the new system.
    • Final system checks and testing to verify that the system is ready for Go-Live.
    • Configuration verification and adjustment based on any final requirements or changes.
  2. Cutover Execution:
    • The specific actions to take place during the transition, such as shutting down legacy systems, migrating data, and activating the new system.
    • Timing of each activity to minimize downtime and business disruption.
  3. Post-Cutover Activities:
    • Monitoring system performance and ensuring stability post Go-Live.
    • Addressing any immediate issues, such as bug fixes or system optimizations.
    • Providing training and support for end-users to ensure they are comfortable with the new system.
  4. Roles and Responsibilities:
    • Clearly defines who is responsible for each task or activity during the cutover process to ensure accountability and smooth execution.
  5. Contingency Planning:
    • Developing backup plans to handle unexpected issues or failures during the cutover process, ensuring that the business can recover quickly.

Importance of a Cutover Plan:

  • Minimizes Downtime: A well-planned cutover ensures that there is minimal disruption to business operations.
  • Ensures Data Integrity: By thoroughly checking data migration and system configurations, the plan ensures that business-critical data remains accurate.
  • Facilitates Smooth System Transition: Clear instructions on roles, tasks, and timing ensure all team members are aligned and that the Go-Live happens as planned.
  • Enhances User Adoption: Post cutover training and support help users get up to speed quickly with the new system.

 

Image 2: Ingredients for a Successful Cut-Over

 

You want to check the available Cutover Template under SAP Activate Roadmap.

In short, a cutover plan is a critical tool for managing the final stages of an implementation project, ensuring a smooth transition and successful adoption of the new system.

Tip: Don't feel overwhelmed, preparation is key, and you'll have an implementation team to guide you in planning and defining the cutover strategy. By understanding what to expect in advance, you'll find it easier to follow the discussions and collaborate effectively with the team throughout the process.

Stay curious and never stop learning!

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Check out other blogs from the series   Customer Onboarding at SAP

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

About the author:

  • Ana Barreira serves as the Global Onboarding Advisor for the SAP Transportation Management (TM) solution within the Supply Chain Management area. She provides expert guidance and support to new SAP customers from contract signature through to successful go-live. With eleven years of experience—three of which have been as a Global Onboarding Advisor, and eight years as an SAP Consultant—Ana specializes in the SAP TM solution. She has successfully supported full-cycle customer implementation projects, assisted partners, and contributed to internal enablement efforts

Having questions, comments or feedbacks? Don't hesitate to comment down below or contact @Ana_Barreira  or @Linh-Hoang .

---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

entry.title: Why choose SAP Business Network Global Track and Trace – handling disparate data sources (2 of 7)
entry.content:
Previously, we explored how #SAP Business Network Global Track and Trace simplifies and empowers supply chain logistics management by enabling a single connection hub for a network of partners, including shippers, carriers, freight forwarders, logistics services providers, and shipment tracking data aggregators.  This is a key benefit of SAP’s solution over other endeavors to gain logistics visibility through multiple different integrations and swivel-chair processes across individual system providers and portals, apart from core ERP and business processes. 

In this post, we delve into the second differentiator described here – handling disparate data sources. 

Reason #2: Handling disparate data sources

This core capability addresses a common pain point for manufacturers and distributers along a global supply chain: the challenge of integrating data from various sources.

Manufacturers and distributers operating along a global supply chain often seek updates from different carriers, freight forwarders, and suppliers, each using separate transportation management systems to track and report on shipment progress. The lack of system data and process standardization among the partners in a supply chain can lead to:

  • Fragmented data within and across organizations
  • Inefficient processes to track shipments in sync with order fulfillment
  • Unreliable estimated times of arrival for inbound PO or outbound sales order deliveries
  • Insufficient information to plan or replan around a risk or realized disruption

Problematic shipment tracking challenges supply chain logistics operations, making any process to track shipments accurately and act on informed decisions very difficult. Trying to reconcile data from multiple sources can be time-consuming, error-prone, and ultimately hinder operational efficiency.  This reduces the value able to be provided by supply chain logistics operations, and increases risk of disruption to inventory management, production schedules, and customer fulfillment and satisfaction. 

SAP's solution tackles this challenge by standardizing and harmonizing incoming process tracking data regardless of the source. This essential solution can provide a unified and consistent view of your entire logistics and fulfillment processes.  Manufacturers can gain not just better visibility, but a deeper, clearer understanding of their supply chain's performance for both inbound and outbound deliveries. By harmonizing data from disparate sources, SAP Business Network Global Track and Trace empowers manufacturers to make data-driven decisions with confidence.

For example, a manufacturer might receive updates from an ocean carrier in one format, while their trucking company uses a completely different system, and their own ERP uses a different data set further still. In this example, how would a manufacturer or distributer keep their shipment and delivery processes in sync with their PO and sales order fulfillment management processes? Without a unified solution, reconciling these updates could require extensive manual effort or costly custom solutions, and even introduce errors putting production and brand reputation at risk. With SAP's solution, shipment tracking, and order fulfillment data is harmonized, providing a single, reliable source of truth for shipment tracking, reliable ETAs, and order management. 

The ability to handle disparate data sources is an essential component of process management, and more so when the process being managed envelopes shipments and fulfillment. Inherent in logistics visibility is network collaboration – essentially, digitized process cooperation among an ecosystem of logistics partners. By providing a unified view of supply chain logistics and fulfillment, SAP Business Network Global Track and Trace empowers global manufacturers to optimize their operations, strengthen their supply chain, and gain valuable insights into their logistics performance. In our next post, we'll explore how this solution goes beyond mere tracking to analyze the impact of disruptions on the business, including customer orders.

 

Read more about how SAP customer Corona harnessed their supply chain logistics ecosystem for operational excellence and customer satisfaction. 

Next up, reason #3: Analyze Impacts.   Check back here for the upcoming link or subscribe to SAP Business Network for Logistics on SAP Community.

entry.title: Track and Trace KITS in the field using S/4 HANA
entry.content:
Pre-requisite:

  • Hospitals must be set up as customers and sales representatives as employees
  • Instruments and Consumables set up separate materials
  • Sales BOM creation to group instruments
  • Install base configuration
  1. Create a KIT in SAP
 

Step 1: Retrieve IBASE number in the front-end field management system. To create a KIT the front-end system will call SAP and create an IBASE. IBASE number would then be communicated back to the source system

Ste2: Front end management system will then use the IBASE number to construct a KIT in the field and relevant items

Step 3: Once the KIT build is complete front-end system will communicate the details to the SAP

Step 4: SAP adds the materials to the IBASE and kicks off the program to create an asset

  1. Add or delete items in a KIT

Instrument KITs can be upgraded by adding new items in the field, below are the steps to accomplish this process

Step 1: Identify KIT and the component to be added. KITs are identified using SAP IBASE number

Step 2: Add the component to the IBASE in the front-end system and communicate the addition to S4

Step 3: S4 Add items to the existing IBASE and create a sub-asset for the added item

 

Conclusion:

  • After going through the above blog, the user should have learned how to fulfill trunk stock requests from medical sales representatives using S4 HANA

 

  • Please provide your feedback/comments. You can ask Question / Answer for the open Questions in the SAP Community here

Reference:

 SAP Help Portal

Authors: Mohan Kaliyamoorthy and Immanuel Godwin Johnson

entry.title: What is SBN GTT and why do I keep hearing about logistics visibility? See the new Product Tour.
entry.content:
Well, below is a long answer describing SAP Business Network Global Track and Trace (SBN GTT), but if you just want to see it...

Check out the new SAP Business Network Global Track and Trace product tour. 

And as for logistics visibility, check out this new series of posts, starting here.

 

What is SAP Business Network Global Track and Trace?

SAP Business Network Global Track and Trace is SAP's solution to provide real-time transportation, logistics, and process visibility.  While highly configurable for tracking any process, the solution is primarily used for tracking visibility into inbound or outbound shipments through a network of connected carriers and logistics services providers.  

Shipments are tracked at the lowest tracking-unit level of detail, such as parcel, truck, or container, across multiple modes of transportation, such as LTL, FTL, ocean, air or rail.  Shipment tracking rolls-up to the tracking of the corresponding highest-level of business document, such as the Purchase Order, Sales Order or Stock Transfer Order.  Therefore, shipment progress and delay impact can be assessed in the context of the business process, to support inventory and production planning or customer service and delivery promise dates.  Shipment and process events, location updates, estimated times of arrival (ETA), and impact assessments are available from within the solution and fed back into ERP and Transportation Management systems to inform related ERP and transportation management processes.  Planned and actual geolocation and route data are ingested directly from carriers and freight forwarders though multiple formats such as API and EDI, or through interoperability with third party transportation data aggregators such as project44, Shippeo, Four Kites, and Transporeon.  

The solution is a single platform for multiple standard and custom tracking scenarios that harmonizes data from multiple disparate sources.  Buyers, manufacturers, and shippers can employ standard business and shipment tracking processes, or change and configure their own custom processes, statuses, event types, or data fields to track.  Examples of alternative tracking scenarios include the tracking of sub-component picking across a multi-warehouse kanban-like manufacturing process or consumption of scope 3 carbon emissions data from a logistics services provider.  

Furthermore, highly configurable alerts and notifications are delivered through multiple channels for “event-to-action” proactive management, delivered to stakeholders within or external to the solution owner's own organization, with links back the source tracking application and information.  Consuming ETA information combined with proactive alerts and notifications for in transit shipments enable solution owners to be predictive in avoiding or mitigating impact from supply chain disruption. 

 

Learn more about SAP Business Network Global Track and Trace and read customer stories here.

entry.title: SAP ATTP. SSCC/SGTIN hierarchy as text and new select options. Idea and steps towards realization
entry.content:
Intro.

Transaction /STTP/Cockpit > Repository Browser > Serialized Containers > Object Hierarchy

The routine daily work of an SAP consultant often includes extracting hierarchies as text. This is necessary for customs declarations, manual EPCIS events, and many other purposes. For example, you might want to extract all SSCC boxes on an SSCC pallet or all SGTINs in an SSCC box (marked as 1 and 2 on the screenshot). However, this cannot be done directly in the transaction /STTP/Cockpit.

Missing but Essential Features

Another must-have feature that is currently missing is the ability to accept multiple inputs in the Transaction /STTP/Cockpit > Repository Browser > Lots, Serialized Trade Items, Serialized Containers, etc. (marked as 1 on the screenshot).

Proposed Solution

Idea

A custom report (and transaction) that supports the following inputs:

  • S_SSCC - SSCC (multiple input supported)

  • S_GTIN - GTIN (multiple input supported)

  • S_SGTIN - SGTIN (multiple input supported)

  • S_LOTNO - Lot number (multiple input supported)

  • S_DOCS - Document numbers (multiple input supported)

Input Options

  • P_EXACT - (Strictly SSCC boxes from the list. Behavior: If checked, the output includes information only about SSCCs in the input. If unchecked, information about all SSCC boxes on the SSCC pallet is included.)

  • P_EWM_F - (Behavior: If checked, the output is without symbols "()".)

  • P_SGTIN - (Behavior: If checked, the output hierarchy is SSCC pallet > SSCC box > SGTINs. If unchecked, the output hierarchy is SSCC pallet > SSCC box.)

  • P_NBRCT - (Behavior: If checked, the output is without segments: (00), (21). If unchecked, the output includes segments (00), (21).)

Output Options

  • P_CONT - Read hierarchy

  • P_STAT - Read statuses

  • P_DOCS - Read assigned documents

For all options, the output is an ALV report with Excel export functionality.

Output for Hierarchy

ALV List: SSCC Pallet, SSCC Box, GS1 GTIN, Lot Number, Qty SGTIN, Trade Item Description

Output for Assigned Statuses

ALV List: Object Code (EPC), Logistic Status, Pack Status, Disposition

Output for Assigned Documents

ALV List: SSCC Pallet, SSCC Box, Assigned Doc. Num

Export to a spreadsheet is available for all outputs (marked as 1 on the screenshot), and MS Excel (*.xlsx) format is also supported (marked as 2 on the screenshot).

Steps Toward Realization

  1. TYPES
    If you are not familiar with data types in the /STTP/ namespace, this collection of types will be a good starting point:
    TYPES.abap

  2. FORM HANDLER
    The START-OF-SELECTION event is raised by the ABAP runtime environment during the execution of an executable program after any standard selection screens have been processed:
     START-OF-SELECTION.abap

  3. DATA MINING
    The class /sttp/cl_dm_query is a central point for custom methods for ATTP data mining:
     ATTP_MISC_CLASS_METHODS

Conclusion

A useful custom report has been introduced, which can significantly aid the routine work of SAP ATTP consultants. If the described use case aligns with your system requirements, you can implement this report in your own system. The UI input/output options and steps for realization have been described in detail.

entry.title: Why choose SAP Business Network Global Track and Trace – Analyzing impacts (3 of 7)
entry.content:
We are hitting our stride with this third post in the series, reaching arguably the most important aspect of SAP Business Network Global Track and Trace when it comes to procurement, supply chain, or customer service.

In the previous installments of this series covering "Why choose SAP Business Network Global Track and Trace over individual visibility platforms," we discussed the core benefits of a unified connection and synchronizing information. Here, we explore a third differentiator: "Analyze Impacts." This key capability elevates shipment tracking from visibility into real business context. 

So, when is logistics visibility not just about shipment tracking?  The answer is when you use real-time shipment tracking to make informed decisions and take action on inventory, production, and customer service. 

Logistics visibility is not just about seeing more, it’s about doing more with what you see. 

Reason #3: Analyze Impacts

For manufacturing and distribution companies in all industries, understanding the ripple effects of disruptions is crucial. Shipment delays, unforeseen events regardless of cause, or changes in demand and customer expectations can all have significant consequences on inventory management and production. Without a direct link between delivery arrival times and purchase, sales, or stock transfer orders, manufacturers risk production delays and inventory imbalances… or worse yet, stock-outs and idle production lines, leading to disappointed customers. 

On the surface, customers want best-price and fast service.  But on another level, customers also just want to know what to expect.  Whether getting your inputs in your door or getting your product out your door is the bigger risk, it is really losing site of how your customers are impacted where you may feel most exposed. 

SAP Business Network Global Track and Trace goes beyond shipment tracking by analyzing the potential impact of delays and disruptions on purchase, sales and stock transfer orders.

Analyzing the impact on the business, natively from you secure system environment, helps you to connect your core processes with what’s going on outside of your company boundaries.  No need to introduce risk, complexity, or inefficiency by sending your core business information, like purchase or sales orders, outside of your secure environment.    

Funneling shipment tracking information, synchronously from multiple sources, back to your core ERP can be essential to:

  • Maintain production schedules. With SAP’s solution, you can quickly pinpoint how delays in raw material shipments affect individual purchase orders down to the line item, thereby impacting production timelines.  Gain the insight to avoid shortages and production bottlenecks.

  • Optimize inventory levels. By analyzing shipment status and ETA for inbound deliveries, relative to planned or actual production and consumption from ERP, manufacturers can proactively manage inventory levels to not only mitigate potential stockouts but also minimize safety stock.  Optimal inventory levels rely on reliable inbound ETAs, else material visibility goes dark while in transit.  

  • Deliver on customer promises. Fulfillment and customer service need to be efficient, agile, and integrated.  Too often these functions rely on “swivel chair” processes, bouncing between multiple systems and external portals, impairing efficiency and agility.  SAP’s solution allows manufacturers and distributers to anticipate potential delays in customer deliveries, integrated across functional areas, enabling customer service functions to natively link sales orders to shipment tracking, to proactively communicate with customers and manage expectations from a single source of truth.

For example, consider a manufacturer experiencing a delay in a critical component shipment due to unforeseen weather conditions. SAP's solution can alert both internal and external stakeholders of the delay in the context of the relevant purchase or stock transfer orders. This analysis might reveal a potential bottleneck in a specific production line, putting a key customer order at risk. Equipped with this insight, the manufacturer can proactively adjust production plans, explore alternative sourcing options, or communicate up to date expectations to their customer, mitigating passing the delay downstream in the supply chain.

The ability to analyze impacts, and put logistics visibility in the native context of business operations, transforms supply chain logistics from a reactive function to a strategic asset. Global manufacturers can move beyond simply knowing where their shipments are to understanding how disruptions might affect their entire operation. This proactive approach empowers them to make informed decisions, optimize resource allocation, and strengthen their overall resilience, for more loyal customers and a stronger brand.

 

Read more about how SAP customer Al Dahra integrated logistics visibility with core processes for greater supply chain orchestration. 

Next up, reason #4: Insight-to-action.  Check back here for the upcoming link or subscribe to SAP Business Network for Logistics on SAP Community.

entry.title: Unlocking Efficiency: The Power of PLM System Integration (PLMSI) by SAP
entry.content:
The future will be shaped by those who effectively integrate design and supply chains. Seamless Integration and collaboration are not just choices but the cornerstones of success in the evolving landscape.

Introduction:

While writing this blog, I am in the SAP services team responsible for North America's PLM integration services & Business development. The views expressed are per my experience working with customers in various industry types like Aerospace & Defense, Hi-tech, Medical/Pharma, Space launch, Automotive, etc.

There is already a considerable amount of valuable information from my colleagues on SAP PLM System Integration (PLMSI) solutions, e.g., videos, blogs like here (new features for the release), or here (architecture principles). This blog will briefly overview the PLMSI product, focusing primarily on its key capabilities that drive innovation and efficiency for our customers.

My primary intent in this blog is to educate you and save you from the pitfalls of custom PLM-SAP product offerings.

Pic 01 - PLMSI overview

Topics covered in this blog:

  1. Overview of PLMSI
  2. Architecture
  3. Key Capabilities – with examples
  4. Best Practices
  5. Myth buster
  6. Key message – Differentiator
  7. Helpful links

1. Overview:

SAP PLMSI is a standard integration solution that connects an external PLM system (Like Siemens Teamcenter, PTC Windchill, 3DX Dassault, or AutoDesk Vault) to SAP for seamless Design to Operate processes, eliminating data silos and increased efficiency.

I want to stress the word "Standard" again, as it is critical to understand its impact & value before any deep dives. As an out-of-the-box (OOTB) solution, it enables synergy between SAP and external PLM systems to enable efficient product data flows and ease collaboration.

Pic 02 - PLMSI Single Source of Product Master Information exchange

Simply put, it saves time and money for your project and Business! Gone are the days of expensive and time-consuming custom PLM-SAP integrations. Not to mention, all the custom PLM add-ons or Project-specific solutions are based on older tech, like RFC calls. They are also not ready for the next-gen SAP business transformations, whether for SAP ECC to S/4 transformations (Private or Public cloud) or even Business AI (what is AI? I never heard the word before 😊).

2. Architecture:

PLMSI is neither a static point-to-point nor a traditional multi-point architecture, which leads to high TCO and deprives customers of quickly adopting the latest innovations, which is a fundamental problem of staying behind.

At SAP, the strategy used for PLMSI is bound to Process level integration- as you conduct different processes in the PLM system, depending on the maturity of data, it will be sent like processes of NPI or Change Management, ultimately driving process & operational efficiency.

Pic 03 - PLMSI process driven integration

As mentioned in the introduction section, I will not focus this blog on architecture; instead, a high-level overview. For those interested in the architecture details, kindly refer to the product site here or blog here.

3. Key Capabilities:

PLM system integration embeds all dimensions of the "Digital Product " from product Eng., Manufacturing engineering, or even Master Data Governance (MDG). Let me help you Zoom into some key master data that makes this OOTB integration unique.

Pic 04 - PLMSI Key capabilities

You can refer to the list of supported object entities on the product site here.

OOTB Configuration control:

Installing the PLMSI component will enable a robust configuration control to manage various settings, including 1:1 mapping, default settings, and even rule-based conversion options.

Pic 04b - PLMSI embedded IMG

No single integration product in the market lets you do that, OOTB!

Let me split the PLMSI-supported business capabilities into two categories:

3a. Foundational Master Data:

Fundamentally, there are specific master data that are critical for the product design, and handover to the downstream ERP system (SAP) is vital for enrichment/or consumption. For example, Material Master & Product Structure (BOM).

To understand the power of SAP PLMSI, let us look at the integration transfer and an example of the underlying SAP PLMSI configuration control power.

Material Master (MM):

Pic 04c - PLMSI Material Master transfer

  • SAP Material master is represented by different names in an external PLM system, e.g., "Product Version" in Siemens Teamcenter & "WTPart" in PTC windchill.
  • In the SAP ERP system, the Material master is more than just an engineering part; it's a financial object holding key information driving different processes around it like warehouse management, Material Requirement Planning, or accounting.

MM transfer example: Below, you see an example of two kinds of attributes:

  1. Mapped 1:1, highlighted(yellow) are attributes mapped from the PLM system (e.g., Siemens Teamcenter) to SAP
  2. Defaulted in SAP, highlighted(blue) are attributes controlled in SAP (Via SAP PLMSI configuration). This does not require any development of the PLM system. Having control of these within SAP is critical specially for master data governance (MDG Link here).
  3. Type and Material Master views, both the type of material (Finish Product- point 6 in screen below) and various views (Basic Data, Storage – Point 8 in screen below).

Pic 04d - PLMSI Material Master e,g, from Siemens Teamcenter

Product Structure (Bill of Material-BOM):

Pic 05a - PLMSI BOM transfer

  • SAP Bill of Material (BOM) is represented by different names in an external PLM system, e.g., it's called "Structure Version" in Siemens Teamcenter & "WTStructure" in PTC windchill.
  • In most straightforward words, a BOM is a list of components and materials required to make a product. BOMs are used in the SAP Production Planning module and other downstream SAP modules.

BOM transfer example: Refer to the below system screenshot

  • Both Header and component attributes are mapped in default SAP PLMSI configuration as a 1:1 Mapping
  • All the key features of BOM can be transferred, may it be BOM-specific component attributes like Production relevancy indicator, Reference designators, Substitutes, or Validity control (both Date and Model Unit Effectivity are supported)

Pic 05b - PLMSI BOM transfer e,g, from Siemens Teamcenter

The same concept applies to other foundational data. For examples:

Document Transfer:

Pic 06a - PLMSI Documents transfer flow

Change control: Bi-directional support!

Pic 06b - PLMSI Change transfer flow

3b. Manufacturing Master Data:

Let's now see some of the Manufacturing relevant Master data needed in the downstream production planning areas. PLMSI supports both classic & version-controlled Master data. I will use the SAP MES system called Product Engineering and Operations (PEO) for this blog (product link here), which is focused on version-controlled data.

P.S. You do not have to have PEO for PLMSI

Pic 07 - PLMSI MES process flow

PLMSI enables seamless Integration into manufacturing areas through its OOTB capabilities. Below is a typical process flow needed for a complex assembly.

 Transfer Example:

This example shows change Record and Routing-It's called the Bill of Process in Siemens Teamcenter and Process Plan in PTC Windchill.

Pic 07b - PLMSI Manufacturing Data Transfer example

Pic 07c - PLMSI Manufacturing Process Plan transfer

The above system screenshot(Pic 07b) shows an example of manufacturing master data transferred to SAP via OOTB PLMSI involving a change packet with MBOM and routing.

From: PLM system

To: SAP S/4

Relevancy

Change Packet (MCN)

Change Record

It acts as a glue to hold all the key information together. One-stop shop for needed data and validity control. It also has a bi-directional support capability.

Production BOM

M-BOM

Plant-relevant BOM linked to a process plan

Bill of Process

Routing

(via PEO Snapshot)

Production Planning-master data holding key information like Operations, work instructions, Inspection characteristics, PRTs, components consumed, etc.

Supported objects:

Below is the total list of business entities supported by OOTB. SAP is invested in this & continuously releases more features. Refer to the roadmap.

Pic 08 - PLMSI Supported Business Entities

Impressive, right? 😊

Takeaway for Key-Capabilities:

  • PLMSI decouples the logic from the PLM system to that of SAP, meaning the system owning attributes controls its logic.
  • It does not require any development on the interface to enable the non-PLM-owned attributes on SAP. This is traditionally different than any custom PLM-integration product in the market! A measure of high TCO on the customer.
  • All dimensions of a digital product are supported by OOTB, ranging from Product Engineering to Manufacturing Master data.

4. Best Practice:

  • Understand the objective - Always start with a clear understanding of business transformation strategy from key stakeholders. This lays the foundation for North Star Vision.
  • Define clear goals and scope - At the end of the discovery phase, the most critical artifact is the attribute mapping file. This should be clearly documented for each in-scope item, laying the foundation for the to-be-built design.
  • Data Quality – The customer should be very active in data for development or testing phases from the beginning. This is one of the primary culprits causing re-do of work.
  • Project Team – This is a niche area; always ask about the team's project experience. I was called into numerous projects that were complicated due to the project team's lack of expertise.

5. Myth-Buster:

The table below captures lists of some of the most common myths & reality about PLMSI Integration.

Pic 09 - PLMSI Myth and Reality

6. Key message & Differentiator

  • Infinite Process Integration – To enable a rapid time to market, processes need to be re-imagined end to end. Integration is no longer a limiting factor in developing more innovative processes within or outside the enterprise.
  • Reduce Cost of Ownership – With an OOTB or simple-to-configure approach, the new Integration enables much less configuration effort and offers reduced complexity to support
  • Flexibility – Enable processes, data models, and software versions to change without affecting the Integration by introducing the meta domain model abstraction layer between the integration components.
  • Cloud Transformation - A technical architecture that supports any deployment model On-Prem, in the cloud, or even in Hybrid Scenarios, with a simple transition between models to allow transformation with significant reinvestment.
  • Strategic –  SAP is dedicated to develop more capabilities our customers need.
  • Choice - With process flexibility and simple transformation, the new Integration enables a customer to choose where processes are executed and where Master Data should reside for the Company's overall benefit.

7. Helpful information:

PLMSI is an SAP product, and below are a few valuable pieces of information

  • SAP PLMSI Product site here
  • Blogs here
  • Admin Guide here
  • Video link here

Reach out to your SAP contact to learn more. Contact me for support in your current or future transformation project. To finish, I want to thank SAP's excellent Product development team for their sincerity and swift resolution of any deployment matters in current or past projects.

Comments or questions are welcome. Thanks for reading my blog. I hope this is worth your time!

entry.title: SAP Integrated Business Planning (SAP IBP) 2502 - Available Now!
entry.content:
Today, January 31st, 2025, SAP Integrated Business Planning 2502 was generally released as planned.

After the successful launch of the early adopter program, we’re excited to announce that Joule in SAP IBP is now generally available.

Note that the use of Joule requires a separate license. Region restrictions may apply. Please check the contractual details with your account manager and see the Joule Onboarding Guide.

In addition to the very first GenAI use case that enables on-the-spot access to assistance and enablement content, we also offer document grounding with Joule. Document grounding allows customers to use their own specific unstructured or semi-structured documents to provide answers to user questions in Joule. Customers can connect their document repositories via a Microsoft SharePoint site. Check out the Getting Started Guide for Document Grounding and the SAP IBP 2408 Joule demo video.

Now let’s explore some of the key features of the first SAP IBP release in 2025:

Foundation

Experience Up to 40% Faster Data Integration with SAP IBP's Cutting-Edge Parallel Processing

In today's fast-paced digital world, efficient data integration is crucial for businesses to stay competitive. SAP IBP's cutting-edge parallel processing technology offers a significant boost, potentially speeding up data integration by up to 40%. This innovative approach allows for simultaneous processing of master and transactional data loads, provided there are no dependencies. Customers with global planning operations and tight batch windows, especially those with multiple divisions and high data frequency, will find this particularly beneficial as it enables seamlessly synchronized decisions based on real-time information, providing superior results.

Best of all, this feature will be automatically enabled for customers, eliminating the need for any additional effort or configuration steps. Overall, customers will experience enhanced performance, reduced bottlenecks, and a significantly more efficient data integration process, driving their business success forward.

Response and Supply

Manage Complex Supply Chain Relations with Real-time Integration of Storage Location MRP Areas

We now offer an out-of-the-box integration of MRP areas from your SAP ECC or SAP S/4HANA backend system to SAP IBP. In the ERP system, an MRP area is an organizational unit within a plant comprising one or more storage locations for which you carry out separate material requirements planning and inventory management. With these storage location MRP areas, you can execute material requirements planning at a more granular level than at the plant level.

You can use real-time integration (RTI) to transfer selected storage location MRP areas to SAP IBP, where they are mapped automatically as independent locations, allowing you to manage complex supply chain relations within a plant without creating additional plants in your ERP backend system.

Demand

Fast and Easy Insights into Selected Forecast Algorithm ​

The winning forecast algorithm of best-fit forecasting can now be stored in a dedicated attribute for further use: Planners can display the forecast algorithm as an additional column directly in the planning UI, they can run statistics on winning forecasting algorithms​, and use the attribute as a filter within planning and analytics use cases​.

With this simplified setup, it's much easier to fine-tune your forecasting and get better insights and usage statistics.

Planning UIs

Mastering Advanced Contextual Navigations in Planner Workspaces

By enabling the use of master data to look up filter values, contextual navigations were further enriched in release 2502. 

Additionally, planners can now also define in which context navigations to another web application or to the SAP GUI show up. For example, you can define that they are only relevant in the context of a specific planning view.  

Best Practices/Analytics Stories

New Preconfigured Analytics Story and Planner Workspace for MRO Planning

​​With SAP IBP 2502, additional Best Practices content for IBP for MRO enables fast adoption of best-in-class analytics ​for the Maintenance & Operations Planning (M&OP) process.

With the new preconfigured analytics story and Planner Workspace, you can instantly analyze the results of the M&OP process steps in an aggregated and detailed format in the web user interface.​ The content enables data-driven decision-making during the MRO planning processes. ​

​The new analytics story for MRO includes visualizations on key measures such as forecast, parts segmentation results, inventory levels, resource utilization. ​

The new Planner Workspace for MRO covers the detailed level of all relevant input and output generated during the M&OP process.

More Information

  • SAP IBP What’s New Webinars 2502
  • 2502 What’s New: Help Portal Documentation
  • What’s New in Best Practices
  • SAP IBP Community Page
  • SAP IBP Roadmap
  • SAP Customer Influence Portal for SAP IBP
  • Questions? Ask here

entry.title: Why choose SAP Business Network Global Track and Trace – Insight to Action (4 of 7)
entry.content:
Throughout this series, based on "Why choose SAP Business Network Global Track and Trace over individual visibility platforms," we've seen how SAP's solution consolidates connections, synchronizes data, and analyzes impacts.
